description: Figure 1 comprises two line graphs illustrating the effect of alloying elements on the physical properties of ferritic iron. Subfigure (a) plots density (g/cm³) versus alloy content (wt%) for ferritic iron alloyed with C, Al, Si, Mn, and Cr. Carbon causes the sharpest drop, reducing density to roughly 7.0 g/cm³ at only ~2 wt%, while Al yields a strong linear decrease to about 6.1 g/cm³ at 16 wt%; Si produces a moderate reduction, and Mn and Cr leave the density nearly unchanged. Subfigure (b) shows Young's modulus (GPa) of annealed Fe-Al steels versus Al content, compiling curves from Frommeyer [24], Kubo [28], Yamamoto-Taniguchi [28], and Fukuroi-Shibuya [28]. All sources report a steady decline from ~215 GPa toward 120–160 GPa as Al rises to 16 wt%. The caption and text state a loss of 2–2.5% per 1 wt% Al, caused by reduced lattice energy and increased Fe-Al atomic spacing. Collectively, the figure highlights that Al effectively decreases density but simultaneously degrades elastic stiffness, a key disadvantage for low-density steel development.
